food_subgroup: Cooked chicken dishes in spiced gravy

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chicken tikka masala; A0CKP (chicken curry, cooked) is the closest defensible analogous anchor in the FoodEx2 hierarchy for a cooked chicken dish in spiced gravy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chicken tikka masala as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage of prepared ethnic dishes is limited.

allergens: may contain gluten (cross-contamination in restaurant kitchens)

serving.description: 1 restaurant portion (curry only, without rice or naan)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of chicken tikka masala in Singapore is approximately 300 g, comprising chicken pieces in gravy, served as a main dish alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka masala, restaurant-style — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken curry with sauce (NDB 35196), cross-referenced with UK Nutrient Databank composite for chicken tikka masala (FSA/PHE McCance and Widdowson 8th edition, food code 5840)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA Agricultural Research Service; UK Food Standards Agency / Public Health England
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source provides a per-100 g entry for restaurant-style chicken tikka masala as served in Singapore. Values are adapted from three complementary approved sources: (1) ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Indian chicken curry macros; (2) USDA SR Legacy NDB 35196 chicken curry with sauce for secondary micronutrients; (3) UK McCance and Widdowson 8th edition food code 5840 (chicken tikka masala, retail/restaurant composite, 150 kcal per 100 g) as the primary anchor, which is the most specific approved match. The spicy restaurant variant served in Singapore is consistent with this composite. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry (GI ~45) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, and University of Sydney GI Database entry for curry dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken tikka masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 40–50 for mixed meat-and-sauce curry dishes); (2) the 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care) which list curry-based dishes in the low-GI range. The dominant glycemic contributors are the onion-tomato gravy and cream, with minimal starchy carbohydrate. GL per serving = (45 × 7.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 9.72, rounded to 9.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving of 300 g)
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of chicken tikka masala provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~7.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken tikka masala has a low estimated GI of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of approximately 9.7 per standard 300 g serving, making it a relatively favourable choice for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from cream and ghee in the gravy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and ghee content of chicken tikka masala contributes approximately 9.6 g of saturated fat per 300 g restaurant serving, which represents nearly half the recommended daily saturated fat limit; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose versions prepared with reduced cream or yoghurt substitution and monitor total portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka Masala is a North Indian-origin dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces that are first marinated in spiced yoghurt and grilled in a tandoor oven to produce charred, smoky tikka, then simmered in a rich, creamy onion-tomato-based gravy seasoned with garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger, garlic, and chilli. The dish has a deep orange-red colour, a velvety texture from the cream or yoghurt finish, and a complex flavour profile that balances smokiness from the tandoor, mild sweetness from the onion-tomato base, and warmth from the spice blend. The spicy variant, as served in Singapore, uses a higher proportion of chilli and garam masala. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat (of which 3.2 g is saturated), and 7.2 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-rich, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index of approximately 45. Clinically, the dish is relatively favourable for blood glucose management due to its low GI and moderate carbohydrate content, but the sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat from cream require attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a main course with naan or basmati rice and is a staple offering at North Indian restaurants. It is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments. The dish is also known as Murgh Tikka Masala in Hindi and is widely recognised by the abbreviation CTM.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken tikka masala?
Is chicken tikka masala good for diabetics?
Is chicken tikka masala high in sodium?

Can I eat chicken tikka masala if I have high blood pressure?
What are the main spices in chicken tikka masala?

ingredient_synonyms.garam masala: warm spice blend (cumin, coriander, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, black pepper)
ingredient_synonyms.tikka: small pieces, typically grilled on skewers
ingredient_synonyms.tandoor: clay oven used for high-heat grilling

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source provides an exact per-100 g entry for restaurant-style chicken tikka masala as served in Singapore. Values are adapted from three complementary approved sources: UK McCance and Widdowson 8th edition (food code 5840, the most specific match), ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, and USDA SR Legacy. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI and GL Values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source (McCance and Widdowson 8th edition food code 5840) nor in ICMR-NIN for this dish category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used. 4. The input food_name 'Chicken Tikka Masala (Spicy) (Chef Recommandation)' has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Chicken Tikka Masala'; the spicy variant and chef recommendation are captured in aliases and description. 5. The description in the input JSON contained typographic run-together words ('grilledintandoor', 'withonion', 'withhot') which have been corrected in the output. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 7. Halal status is marked true as the dish is chicken-based and served at Indian Muslim-friendly restaurants in Singapore, but individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest analogous anchor; a dedicated FoodEx2 code for chicken tikka masala does not exist in the publicly available FoodEx2 browser as of the accessed date.
